meta.title: Desert People
meta.author: David Meissner
meta.focusQuestion: How have different peoples adapted to survive in the world's harsh deserts?

sections.title: Demanding Deserts
paragraphs: Imagine that you are standing alone in the desert before dawn. There is total silence except for the soft call of a dove. The Sun begins to rise over the mountains, which slowly change from black to lavender. Then the Sun's rays peek out and turn the morning into day.

Look around you. Imagine that your family and friends also live here. What are your lives like? Imagine, as was true a few hundred years ago, that there are no cars, bicycles, or paved roads in this desert. How do you travel from place to place?

Imagine that you feel hungry in this desert. There are no stores or supermarkets. Can you feel the thirst in the back of your throat? Where can you find water to drink? You look around. There are no drinking fountains and no flowing rivers. There is not a single cloud in the light blue sky.

What Exactly Is a Desert? Would you recognize a desert if you saw one? Would there be camels walking on sand dunes? Would there be rattlesnakes, tall mountains, and saguaro cacti? Or would the landscape be rocky, desolate, and barren, with only a few shrubs and trees?
All three of these descriptions apply to different kinds of deserts. Deserts may contain rocky mountains, graveled plateaus, or rolling sand dunes. Some desert areas even have streams and rivers.

There are many varieties of desert environments, but all of them share some common traits. Compared with the rest of the world, deserts are arid places. They usually receive less than 25.4 centimeters (10 in) of precipitation each year. Deserts tend to have dry soil and not much vegetation. Although some deserts can get very cold, most deserts are sunny and hot.
If deserts are such inhospitable places, how have people survived in them? Where did they find food to eat and water to drink? What materials did they use to build shelter from the elements?

For thousands of years, desert people have found answers to these questions. They have used local resources for food, water, and home construction. Desert people have found ways to adapt. Each desert presents different challenges, and each desert has its own solutions.

sections.title: The Tohono O'odham
paragraphs: Tohono O'odham means "desert people." The Tohono O'odham are Native Americans who still live in the Sonoran Desert in the southwestern United States.

For years, they have watched the Sun rise over Baboquivari, their sacred mountain. They believe that Baboquivari is where Elder Brother created them from the clay of the Earth. Like many Native Americans, the O'odham believe that a powerful spirit named Coyote helped create the people and the land. They believe Elder Brother gave the desert to the O'odham and taught them how to use its plants.

The O'odham lived in villages and slept in round houses with flat roofs. These homes were made out of a plaster of dried mud, branches, and grass called adobe. The O'odham also built covered shelters called ramadas to create shaded areas near their homes.

The Sonoran Supermarket: Like other deserts, the Sonoran Desert can be a difficult place to live. It is hot and dry. Months may pass without any rain, and summer days can reach 49 degrees Celsius (120 degrees F). Winter nights can dip below freezing.

But compared to other deserts, the Sonoran is relatively lush and bountiful. Sometimes it rains in the winter, and monsoons drench the entire desert with water every summer. During the monsoon season, flash floods sometimes race down the canyons!

Historically, the Tohono O'odham lived in an area where 30.5 centimeters (12 in) of rain fell each year. Enough rain fell to support many forms of life. Shady trees, big cacti such as the saguaro, and even summer flowers all grew there. Insects, birds, rabbits, and coyotes lived there, too.

Some of the O'odham lived near large mountain ranges. In the mountains, they found resources lacking in the desert below. Different plants and animals lived up in the cooler climate on the mountain slopes. In the mountains, the O'odham gathered food and hunted large game animals. They also collected water from springs and streams.

If the Tohono O'odham had a supermarket, it was their desert. When they needed vegetables, they planted seeds. When they needed water, they went to a spring. When they needed meat, they hunted animals. And if they needed pine nuts, they walked up into the mountains to find pine trees.

In the early summer, O'odham men dug small holes in the ground with sharp sticks. Women dropped seeds in the holes and covered them up. The dry fields flooded when the monsoons came. The O'odham grew corn, pinto beans, and squash in this way. In the winter, they grew wheat, peas, and watermelons.

The Tohono O'odham hunted rattlesnakes, rabbits, and birds in the desert. In the mountains, they killed deer, bighorn sheep, and sometimes even bears. When sneaking up on the deer, O'odham hunters dressed in deerskins and walked on all fours to blend in with the animals.

The Tohono O'odham also gathered food that grew in the wild. In the desert, they found chile peppers, wild onions, mesquite beans, and fruit from saguaro and other cacti. They made syrup from the fruit. In the mountains, they collected acorns, roots, and pine nuts. O'odham men even made four-day trips to the ocean just to get salt!

Some O'odham villages were lucky enough to be located near springs and streams. Other villages dug wells in the ground. But many people lived far away from water sources. Young girls often hiked high up into mountain canyons to fetch water.
The O'odham created wonderful baskets from the leaves of the yucca plant. They weaved beautiful designs into the bottoms of the baskets. These baskets were tight enough to hold water.

sections.title: The Bedouin
paragraphs: Think of all the things in your room. Can you imagine packing up and moving to a new place every week? Would you get tired of carrying so many things? How would you enjoy the traveling life?

Traditional Bedouin people were nomadic. They moved from place to place on camels in search of food, water, and grazing land. They also rode horses and raised sheep and goats. The Bedouin had very few possessions; they believed their lifestyle was simple and pure. Bedouin means "people of the desert," but it is a word that French people created. Bedouins call themselves Badw.

Today, most Bedouins are Muslims, but long ago they had different beliefs that were tied to the landscape they lived in. Water was considered sacred, and so were caves. Ancient Bedouins saw kind gods in fertile places and cruel demons, called jinn, where it was desolate. They worshipped the Moon and the evening star, Venus.

The Bedouin lived in low black tents called bayts, which were made out of goat hair or sheep's wool. These tents protected them from the sun, wind, and blowing sand. In a matter of hours, Bedouins could pack up their camps and move on to their next "home." Today, small numbers of Bedouins still roam the Syrian, Arabian, and Sahara deserts. In some ways, they live exactly as their ancestors did five thousand years ago.

The Saharan Supermarket? The vast Sahara Desert is the largest desert in the world. It stretches across northern Africa for 9 million square kilometers (3.5 million sq mi). The Sahara is hot and dry. In some parts, it rains less than 25 millimeters (1 in) per year! The Sahara has more sandstorms than rainstorms; huge clouds of dust and sand can fill the sky.

The Sahara Desert is less of a "supermarket" than the Sonoran Desert. Less rain falls in the Sahara, and fewer plants and animals live there. With few local resources available to them, the Bedouin could not just remain in one place. They had to constantly travel the desert in search of food and water.

Ancient Bedouins were used to eating and drinking less than other people. One secret to living in the desert was to wear light layers of white clothing. These clothes helped Bedouins remain cool under the hot sun and helped their bodies retain much-needed moisture.

The Saharan landscape contains more than sand dunes. It also has rocky plateaus, tall mountains, valleys, and oases. An oasis — a rich, fruitful place in the desert with water — was sacred to the Bedouins.

Arabian camels, known as dromedaries, made nomadic life possible. The Bedouin wove camel hair into fabrics out of which they created beautiful blankets and heavy winter coats. They also milked the camels. Bedouin women knew how to turn that milk into yogurt or ghee (a kind of butter). Bedouins also ate camel meat. They even burned camel dung to fuel their campfires!

Camels were well suited for the desert. They could go for long periods of time without water. They stored fat in their humps. Camels could drink amazing amounts of water at one time — a useful behavior when one might not have another drink for days. Arabian camels could walk 50 kilometers (30 mi) a day while carrying up to 270 kilograms (600 lbs) on their backs.

Some families owned many camels, which was considered a sign of wealth. Camels were so prized that people fought over them. Some Bedouins went on raids to steal camels from others. Bedouins were very good at raiding, and for thousands of years, other desert peoples were afraid of them. In the desert, people traveled in caravans for safety. If a caravan wanted to pass through Bedouin territory, it might have to pay a toll or hire Bedouin guards for protection.

The nomadic Bedouin did not grow many crops because they were always on the move. But in late summer, they camped out near oases because date palms grew there. These palm trees produced sweet, tasty fruits. The Bedouin ate some dates fresh and dried others to save for later.

Bedouins did not eat meat very often. Since their camels were valuable, they only ate them on special occasions. They also ate sheep and goats during celebrations. The Bedouin hunted ostriches, wolves, foxes, and gazelles. They also trained falcons to hunt foxes and small birds for them. Sometimes dogs called Salukis rode with Bedouins to help track and hunt animals.
The Bedouin knew where to find the desert oases and where to dig. They dug wells to extract water from the ground.

sections.title: More Desert People
paragraphs: The Tohono O'odham and the Bedouin are just two of the world's traditional desert peoples. There are many other deserts, and there have been many other desert peoples.

The Australian Aborigines, for example, walked about their continent for millennia, moving from water source to water source. They learned songs that told them where to find water in the desert, which covers most of the continent. Men hunted kangaroos, bandicoots, many kinds of birds, and other animals with spears and boomerangs. Women gathered vegetables such as acacia seeds, bush tomatoes, desert limes, and bush bananas. The Australian Aboriginal diet also included roots, insects, and grubs.

Nomads roamed the cold, windy Gobi Desert in Mongolia and China. They were sometimes called the "five-animal people" because they traditionally herded horses, sheep, goats, cattle, and two-humped Bactrian camels. They lived in round tents called gers. They ate cheese, meat, and curds made from goat or camel milk. Four times a year, these nomads would pack their gers and other belongings and move to a different area to find food for their animals.

The Atacama Indians lived in the dry Atacama Desert plateau in Chile. The Atacama is one of the driest areas in the world. Much of the area has not received any rain for hundreds of years. It is also very cold because it is so high in the mountains. The native Atacama people raised guinea pigs, llamas, and alpacas. They also planted corn, beans, and squash in areas with water from melting snow. Though it rarely rains in the Atacama, there are some areas where thick fogs rise up from the Pacific Ocean far below. Native people harvested water from these fogs by using funnels to capture the morning dew.

Bushmen lived in the Kalahari Desert in southern Africa. They traveled in small bands and moved around frequently, following the animals they hunted for food. They sometimes stored water inside empty ostrich eggshells. They lived in huts made of branches tied together and covered with thatched grass. Women gathered berries and roots. Melon was a favorite food, and honey was a treat. Men hunted animals with bows and poison-tipped arrows. Sometimes they ate giraffes!

sections.title: A Way of Life
paragraphs: Imagine again that you are standing alone in the desert. The Sun is now shining over the mountains. The doves have found shade under a tree. You feel a bead of sweat on your forehead. What will you do on this day?

If you grew up in the desert, you would know exactly what to do. Your grandparents would have taught your parents. And your parents would have prepared you with many generations' worth of knowledge from your ancestors.
For thousands of years, people have adapted to deserts. They have found food, water, shelter, and more. For these people, the desert is not just about survival. It is their way of life.

vocabulary.sentence: Desert people have found ways to adapt.

options: leave a place because it is too harsh
build permanent shelters in the desert

vocabulary.sentence: Your parents would have prepared you with many generations' worth of knowledge from your ancestors.

options: children who will live in the future

vocabulary.sentence: Compared with the rest of the world, deserts are arid places.

vocabulary.definition: the weather conditions in an area over a long period of time
vocabulary.sentence: Different plants and animals lived up in the cooler climate on the mountain slopes.

options: the type of soil found in an area
the amount of rain falling on a single day
the weather conditions in an area over a long period of time
the kinds of animals that live in a region

vocabulary.definition: traveling from place to place without a permanent home

options: living in large villages with many people
staying in one place for your whole life
hunting animals with bows and arrows
traveling from place to place without a permanent home

vocabulary.definition: supplies of valuable or very useful things
vocabulary.sentence: They have used local resources for food, water, and home construction.

options: supplies of valuable or very useful things
buildings where people live and work
types of desert animals and plants
tools used for farming and hunting

vocabulary.sentence: The vast Sahara Desert is the largest desert in the world.

options: very small and easy to miss

vocabulary.sentence: Deserts tend to have dry soil and not much vegetation.

options: rocks and soil in an area
animals that live in the desert

vocabulary.sentence: But compared to other deserts, the Sonoran is relatively lush and bountiful.

vocabulary.definition: fertile places in the desert that have water
vocabulary.sentence: An oasis — a rich, fruitful place in the desert with water — was sacred to the Bedouins.

options: large sand dunes in the desert
fertile places in the desert that have water
seasonal storms that bring heavy rain

vocabulary.sentence: The Australian Aborigines, for example, walked about their continent for millennia.

vocabulary.definition: seasonal storms that bring heavy rains to an area
vocabulary.sentence: Sometimes it rains in the winter, and monsoons drench the entire desert with water every summer.

options: dry winds that blow sand across the desert
seasonal storms that bring heavy rains

questions.question: What is one way the Sonoran Desert is different from the Sahara Desert?
questions.evidence: Historically, the Tohono O'odham lived in an area where 30.5 centimeters (12 in) of rain fell each year. The Sahara... in some parts, it rains less than 25 millimeters (1 in) per year!
choices: The Sonoran Desert is home to the Bedouin
The Sonoran Desert has fewer animals
The Sonoran Desert has fewer plants
The Sonoran Desert gets more rain per year
questions.explanation: The Sonoran gets about 12 inches of rain per year while parts of the Sahara get less than 1 inch — the Sonoran gets much more rain!

questions.question: What made it possible for the Tohono O'odham to grow plants in the Sonoran Desert?
questions.evidence: The dry fields flooded when the monsoons came. The O'odham grew corn, pinto beans, and squash in this way.
choices: the high elevation where they lived
the rich soils near springs
the summer rains and monsoons
the warm winter nights
questions.explanation: The O'odham planted seeds in holes, and when the summer monsoons came, the flooding water helped their crops — corn, beans, and squash — grow.

questions.question: Why did people living in different deserts eat different foods?
questions.evidence: Each desert presents different challenges, and each desert has its own solutions. Different plants and animals lived in each desert environment.
choices: People in different deserts simply liked different foods
Different foods were available in each desert environment
People didn't know about what other deserts had
People didn't know which plants were safe to eat
questions.explanation: Each desert had different plants and animals. Desert people used whatever resources were available in their own environment — so their diets varied.

questions.question: Why did Tohono O'odham girls need to be strong?
questions.evidence: But many people lived far away from water sources. Young girls often hiked high up into mountain canyons to fetch water.
choices: Because they had to carry water over long distances
Because they needed to protect their villages from enemies
Because they built the adobe houses by themselves
Because they hunted large animals in the mountains
questions.explanation: Many villages were far from water sources, so young girls had to hike high into mountain canyons to fetch water — carrying it back over long distances required strength.

questions.question: Why did the author call the Sonoran Desert like a "supermarket" for the Tohono O'odham?
questions.evidence: If the Tohono O'odham had a supermarket, it was their desert. When they needed vegetables, they planted seeds. When they needed water, they went to a spring. When they needed meat, they hunted animals.
choices: The O'odham could pay cash to other tribes for food
The O'odham often saw their neighbors while hunting
The O'odham could find almost all the food they needed somewhere in the desert
The O'odham could ride camels to the next village's grocery store
questions.explanation: The desert provided almost everything the O'odham needed — vegetables, water, meat, pine nuts, salt, and more — just like a supermarket has everything you need.

questions.question: How did the Bedouin solve the problem of not having enough water?
questions.evidence: The Bedouin knew where to find the desert oases and where to dig. They dug wells to extract water from the ground.
choices: They only lived near rivers and streams
They bought bottled water from trading caravans
They collected rainwater in large containers
They knew where to find oases and dug wells to extract water
questions.explanation: The Bedouin knew where to find desert oases (natural water sources) AND they dug wells to extract water from underground — two methods working together.

questions.question: How was the Sahara Desert's "supermarket" different from the Sonoran Desert's "supermarket"?
questions.evidence: The Sahara Desert is less of a "supermarket" than the Sonoran Desert. Less rain falls in the Sahara, and fewer plants and animals live there.
choices: The Sahara was more important to the Bedouin than the Sonoran was to the O'odham
The Sahara had fewer kinds of plants and animals available
The Sahara had more animals and water sources
The Sahara was smaller than the Sonoran Desert
questions.explanation: The Sahara received less rain than the Sonoran, so it had fewer plants and animals — it was a less "bountiful" supermarket.

questions.question: If you "adapt" to a situation, it means you:
questions.evidence: Desert people have found ways to adapt. They have used local resources for food, water, and home construction. (Glossary: adapt — to adjust to new conditions)
choices: leave the area
adjust to new conditions
build permanent homes
travel to find food
questions.explanation: To adapt means to adjust to new conditions. Desert people adapted by finding ways to use local resources for survival.

questions.question: Why was it important for the Bedouin to own animals that could carry heavy loads?
questions.evidence: Traditional Bedouin people were nomadic. They moved from place to place on camels in search of food, water, and grazing land.
choices: The Bedouin often moved to new areas with all their belongings
The Bedouin needed camels to help dig wells
The Bedouin were very big people who needed strong animals
The Bedouin were unable to keep weak animals alive
questions.explanation: Because the Bedouin were nomadic (always moving), they needed camels to carry their belongings as they traveled from place to place in search of resources.

questions.question: Why did the author most likely write this book?
questions.evidence: The book describes how different desert peoples — the Tohono O'odham, the Bedouin, Australian Aborigines, Gobi nomads, Atacama Indians, and Bushmen — adapted to their environments.
choices: to explain the customs of only the Tohono O'odham
to tell about the different foods found in a desert
to give people information about traveling to deserts
to describe how different peoples learned to live in desert areas
questions.explanation: The author wrote this book to show how various peoples around the world — each in a different desert — adapted and found ways to survive using local resources.

questions.question: How have people survived in deserts? Use information from the book to support your answer.
questions.explanation: Use examples from the book — how people found food, water, and shelter using local resources.
opinionPrompts: What natural resources did desert people use for food and water?
How did different groups adapt to their specific desert?
What evidence from the text supports your answer?

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Deserts are arid places that receive very little precipitation, but people have survived in them for thousands of years by using local resources and adapting to their environments.
keyDetails: Deserts may have rocky mountains, plateaus, sand dunes, or even streams
All deserts receive less than 25.4 cm (10 in) of precipitation per year
Deserts tend to have dry soil and not much vegetation
Desert people have found ways to adapt using local resources
distractors: The Tohono O'odham lived in adobe houses
Bedouin people lived in tents called bayts

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: The Tohono O'odham used the Sonoran Desert as their "supermarket," finding food, water, and materials for shelter by farming with monsoons, hunting, gathering wild plants, and collecting water from springs and mountain canyons.
keyDetails: The Sonoran Desert gets enough rain (12 in/year) to support many forms of life
The O'odham grew corn, beans, and squash using monsoon flooding
They hunted in the desert and mountains, gathering wild foods like chile peppers and pine nuts
Young girls hiked into mountain canyons to fetch water when villages were far from sources
distractors: Bedouin people were nomadic and lived in tents
Australian Aborigines learned songs to find water
transitionWords: If the Tohono O'odham had a supermarket

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: The Bedouin were nomadic people of the Sahara who relied on camels for transportation, food, and clothing, constantly traveling in search of water from oases and wells.
keyDetails: The Bedouin were nomadic — moving from place to place on camels
Camels provided meat, milk, hair for fabric, and could carry heavy loads
The Sahara is a harsher, drier "supermarket" than the Sonoran Desert
The Bedouin knew where to find oases and dug wells to extract water
distractors: The O'odham grew corn using monsoon rains
Gobi nomads lived in round tents called gers

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Many other cultures around the world have adapted to desert life — including Australian Aborigines, Gobi nomads, Atacama Indians, and Kalahari Bushmen — each using their environment's unique resources.
keyDetails: Australian Aborigines used songs to find water and hunted with spears and boomerangs
Gobi nomads herded five types of animals and moved four times a year
Atacama Indians harvested water from fog using funnels
Kalahari Bushmen stored water in ostrich eggshells and hunted with poison-tipped arrows
distractors: The O'odham made baskets from yucca leaves that could hold water
Bedouin trained falcons to hunt small birds

demandingDeserts.title: Arid & Vegetation
demandingDeserts.content: Arid = very dry. Vegetation = plants. Deserts have little rain (arid) and few plants (vegetation). Less than 10 inches of rain per year!

demandingDeserts.title: Compare & Contrast
demandingDeserts.content: All deserts share: arid, <25.4 cm rain/year, dry soil, little vegetation. But they DIFFER: some have mountains, some sand dunes, some even have streams!

demandingDeserts.title: Main Idea
demandingDeserts.content: Key question: How have people survived in inhospitable deserts? Answer: They ADAPTED by using local resources for food, water, and shelter.

tohono.title: Monsoons
tohono.content: Monsoons = seasonal storms bringing heavy rain. The O'odham PLANTED seeds in dry fields, then monsoons FLOODED them — nature's irrigation system!

tohono.title: Desert = Supermarket
tohono.content: The Sonoran Desert was like a supermarket: vegetables → plant seeds; water → go to spring; meat → hunt animals; pine nuts → walk to mountains!

tohono.title: Compare: Sonoran vs. Sahara
tohono.content: Sonoran: 12 in rain/year, lush, bountiful. Sahara: <1 in rain/year, less plants, fewer animals. Sonoran = better supermarket!

bedouin.title: Nomadic & Oases
bedouin.content: Nomadic = traveling without permanent home. Oases = fertile places with water in the desert. Bedouin moved to find oases and dug wells!

bedouin.title: Why Camels?
bedouin.content: Camels = everything! Hair → fabric. Milk → yogurt/butter. Meat → food. Dung → fuel. Could walk 30 mi/day carrying 600 lbs. Store fat in humps!

bedouin.title: Compare & Contrast
bedouin.content: Both O'odham and Bedouin used desert as 'supermarket.' DIFFERENCE: Sonoran had more resources → O'odham stayed in villages. Sahara had fewer resources → Bedouin had to keep moving (nomadic).

morePeople.title: Millennia
morePeople.content: Millennia = thousands of years. Australian Aborigines walked their continent for MILLENNIA, moving from water source to water source!

morePeople.title: Four More Desert Peoples
morePeople.content: 1) Australian Aborigines: songs → find water, hunted with boomerangs. 2) Gobi nomads: 5 animals, lived in gers. 3) Atacama: fog → water. 4) Bushmen: ostrich eggs → water storage.

wayOfLife.title: Ancestors
wayOfLife.content: Ancestors = family from long ago. Desert knowledge passed down: grandparents → parents → you. Thousands of years of adaptation!

wayOfLife.title: Main Idea
wayOfLife.content: For these people, desert life isn't just about survival — it's their WAY OF LIFE. Thousands of years of knowledge from ancestors!

grammar.title: Prepositions
grammar.explanation: Prepositions show the relationship between things. They tell WHERE, WHEN, or HOW something happens. For example: "in the desert" tells WHERE, "before dawn" tells WHEN.
rules: Common prepositions: in, on, at, over, under, across, between, near, from, to, into, through, around, before, after, during, with, without
Prepositions show WHERE: "in the desert", "on camels", "across northern Africa"
Prepositions show WHEN: "before dawn", "for thousands of years", "in late summer"
Prepositions show HOW: "with spears and boomerangs", "on all fours"

exercises.sentence: Imagine that you are standing alone in the desert before dawn.
exercises.hint: WHERE are you standing? WHEN is it?

exercises.sentence: The Sun begins to rise over the mountains, which slowly change from black to lavender.
exercises.hint: WHERE does the Sun rise? What colors does it change BETWEEN?

exercises.sentence: Traditional Bedouin people moved from place to place on camels in search of food, water, and grazing land.
answers: in search of food, water, and grazing land
exercises.hint: WHERE did they move? HOW did they travel? WHY did they move?

exercises.sentence: Young girls often hiked high up into mountain canyons to fetch water.
exercises.hint: WHERE did the girls hike? Find the prepositional phrase.

exercises.question: Which word is the preposition in this sentence?
exercises.sentence: The vast Sahara Desert stretches across northern Africa.
exercises.explanation: "Across" is the preposition — it shows WHERE the Sahara stretches (across northern Africa).

exercises.question: What does the preposition tell us in "lived in villages"?
exercises.sentence: The O'odham lived in villages and slept in round houses.
exercises.explanation: "In villages" tells us WHERE the O'odham lived — it shows location.

exercises.sentence: The Bedouin lived in low black tents made _____ goat hair or sheep's wool.
exercises.explanation: "Made out of" tells us what material the tents were made from — goat hair or sheep's wool.

exercises.sentence: For thousands of years, people have adapted _____ deserts.
exercises.explanation: We say "adapted to" — people adjusted TO the desert conditions.

exercises.prompt: How do you read this temperature aloud?
exercises.answer: forty-nine degrees Celsius, one hundred twenty degrees Fahrenheit
exercises.explanation: Read the number first: "forty-nine", then the unit: "degrees Celsius".

exercises.prompt: How do you read this measurement aloud?
exercises.answer: thirty point five centimeters, twelve inches
exercises.explanation: Read 30.5 as "thirty point five" — not "thirty and five"!

exercises.prompt: How do you read this area aloud?
exercises.explanation: Read the number: "nine million", then the unit: "square kilometers".

exercises.prompt: How do you read this measurement aloud?
